blocks.text: Then Job answered:
“How you have helped one who has no power! How you have assisted the arm that has no strength!
How you have counseled one who has no wisdom, and given much good advice!
With whose help have you uttered words, and whose spirit has come forth from you?
The shades below tremble, the waters and their inhabitants.
26.5 : , i.e., the inhabitants of the underworld.
Sheol is naked before God, and Abaddon has no covering.
6 : (meaning “destruction”), another name for Sheol, the abode of the dead.
He stretches out Zaphon over the void, and hangs the earth upon nothing.
7 : At creation, God stretched out the heavens (here called , “the north”) like a tent without a center pole and suspended the earth from .
He binds up the waters in his thick clouds, and the cloud is not torn open by them.
8 : The are conceived as waterskins (cf. 38.37).
He covers the face of the full moon, and spreads over it his cloud.
He has described a circle on the face of the waters, at the boundary between light and darkness.
10 : The appears to be both the horizon where the sky meets the earth and the boundary between day and night.
The pillars of heaven tremble, and are astounded at his rebuke.
11 : The are envisaged as supporting the solid canopy of heaven; they are the same as the “foundations of the heavens” (2 Sam 22.8).
By his power he stilled the Sea; by his understanding he struck down Rahab.
12 : The depiction is still of the creation of the world, when according to some biblical myths God waged a battle against a monster known as or (cf. 9.13n.; Ps 89.11; Isa 51.9).
By his wind the heavens were made fair; his hand pierced the fleeing serpent.
13 : The is another of God’s primordial enemies, called Leviathan in 3.8 and Isa 27.1.
These are indeed but the outskirts of his ways; and how small a whisper do we hear of him! But the thunder of his power who can understand?”
3.1–42.6: The dialogues.
21.1–27.23: Third cycle of speeches.

25.1–26.14: Bildad’s third speech. As it stands, Bildad’s speech is uncommonly short (25.2–6) but although 26.1–14 is now assigned to Job, it was probably originally part of Bildad’s speech. Such a rearrangement would help remove the problem of having three speeches of Job in a row (26, – , 29–31) without any intervening words from the friends. The theme of Bildad’s speech would then be the power of God, in three spheres: in the heavens (25.2–6), in the underworld (26.5–6), and in creation (26.7–13). The speech is an address to Job (cf. 25.4,6; 26.14), belittling him (26.2–4), denying his claim to innocence (25.2–6), and putting him in his place by focusing exclusively on God’s untrammeled power (26.5–14).

14 : These visible and reputed proofs of God’s greatness are but the , and convey only a faint of the of his actual power. Humans cannot hope to comprehend the real God, but can catch only a glimpse of him.
